snippets.title: RMSE and MAE from residuals
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

y_true = np.array([10.0, 12.0, 8.0])
y_pred = np.array([9.0, 15.0, 8.5])

residuals = y_true - y_pred
mse = np.mean(residuals ** 2)
rmse = np.sqrt(mse)
mae = np.mean(np.abs(residuals))

print("MSE:", mse)
print("RMSE:", rmse)
print("MAE:", mae)
snippets.explanation: RMSE = √MSE on held-out pairs. The large residual at index 1 (error 3) raises RMSE more than MAE.

snippets.title: sklearn metrics on test data
snippets.code:
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error, root_mean_squared_error
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import numpy as np

X = np.random.randn(500, 3)
y = X @ np.array([2.0, -1.0, 0.5]) + np.random.randn(500) * 0.5

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=0)

model = LinearRegression().fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)

print("test RMSE:", root_mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred))
print("test MAE:", mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred))
print("test MSE:", mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred))
snippets.explanation: Fit on train, score on test only. sklearn exposes RMSE directly (root_mean_squared_error) or via np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(...)).

snippets.title: Compare two models on the same split
snippets.code:
def regression_report(y_true, y_pred, label):
    mae = np.mean(np.abs(y_true - y_pred))
    rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean((y_true - y_pred) ** 2))
    print(f"{label}: MAE={mae:.3f}, RMSE={rmse:.3f}")

# Same y_test, different predictions
regression_report(y_test, y_pred, "linear model")
regression_report(y_test, np.full_like(y_test, y_train.mean()), "mean baseline")
snippets.explanation: Always compute RMSE and MAE on the same held-out split when comparing models or baselines.
